issue_title: scan() cannot raise exception in case of not all shards being successful
issue_body:
In the case of a RED status index, we do not get "failed" shards, we just get not all shards being successful e.g. JSON snippet below:

"_shards":{"total":20,"successful":7,"failed":0}

The scan() method allows a raise_on_exception parameter but this has the following issues:

1) It only raises if a shard is in the "failed" state but cannot be used to raise when not all shards were successful

2) It is only checked after yielding all successful docs, potentially meaning you scan through a huge number of docs and only get the exception at the end, when the server indicates shard status immediately in the HTTP response and so the exception could be raise immediately in principle.

3) The response data is not accessible to the caller so it is not possible to check the shard status without re-implementing the entire method.

It would be great if the functionality could include the following:

1) raise_on_error can cause an exception when not all shards are successful
2) Exception is raised immediately before yielding docs

Relevant code is linked below:

https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/helpers/__init__.py#L374-L388

issue_title: [Discussion] Would it be valuable to allow configuring a default resource group?
issue_body:
I wonder whether it wouldn't be valuable to allow users to configure a default resource group, either per-user, per-shell or per-directory (via some kind of config file), and would take effect for all commands where a resource group wasn't explicitly specified. I've seen this kind of behavior provided by other CLIs, with the goal of reducing the number of repetitive parameters, especially if they don't change frequently or are context-sensitive (e.g. can be derived via a config file in the CWD).

I'm not entirely sure how devs use resource groups in the wild, but it seems like it could be a useful simplification of their workflow by being able to configure a resource group for a session, project, etc. and then omit it from there on out. That way the CLI can strike a balance between flexibility and verbosity.

lots of fiddling around, I used a new container just to experiment with,
and to my surprise, it looks like `--dryrun` just ignores these flags,

to use a dry run flag to see what will be deleted, and ignoring these
flags means that I can end up deleting blobs unexpectedly.

issue_title: Metahooks
issue_body:
I'd like to be able to configure pre-commit to run a hook, which itself specifies one or more other hooks to run, along with their configuration.

Consider the case of an organization with multiple repositories which wants to enforce a single `yapf` configuration between them.  Today, each repository has to include something like the following stanza in its configuration file:
```
-   repo: git://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-yapf
    sha: v0.21.0
    hooks:
    -   id: yapf
        args:
        - -i
        - --style=...
```

Instead, it would be useful to be able to have a git repository with an entry in its `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` which looks something like this:
```
-   id: my-org-hooks
    name: Hooks for my organization
    metahooks:
    -   repo: git://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-yapf
        sha: v0.21.0
        hooks:
        -   id: yapf
            args:
            - -i
            - --style=...
```

The individual projects could then just put the following in their `.pre-commit-config.yaml` files:
```
-   repo: git://myrepo.git
    sha: ...
    hooks:
    -   id: my-org-hooks
```

In this case, `my-org-hooks` would run each of the entries in `hooks` as a separate hook with the given arguments, reporting the results independently, perhaps with the following output:
```
Hook 1...............................................(no files to check)Skipped
Hook 2...................................................................Passed
Hooks for my organization:
  yapf...................................................................Passed
```

Any organization-wide changes could then be applied to the centralized location, rather than having to be changed everywhere.

issue_title: Autopep8 doesn't respect pep8 section in setup.cfg
issue_body:
Since https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8/pull/167 autopep8 has started reading the ```pep8``` section from ```tox.ini``` or ```setup.cfg``` of a project. Any way we could get this to work?

issue_title: flatten / expand
issue_body:
What's the use case for `flatten()` and `expand()`? Do we want to keep them?

`ListType` has a weird feature specifically to support `expand`: it accepts dictionaries and uses their keys to sort the values. This is so that the list

```
field = ['foo', 'bar']
```

can be flattened into

```
{'field.0': 'foo', 'field.1': 'bar'}
```

and successfully expanded without confusing it with a dictionary with keys 0 and 1.

I'm not okay with `ListType` accepting dictionaries. To work around this, I taught `expand` to interpret a flattened structure with a zero-based key sequence as a list. This works, but it means that a legitimate dictionary with (only) zero-based integer keys will also become a list on expansion. Is this good enough? Does anyone use `flatten`?

The alternative is to rewrite `expand()` so that it actually looks at the model it's expanding to, but I don't want to go there if this is just a vestigial feature that no one uses.

issue_title: is there a fragile dependence on garbage collection?
issue_body:
This test works on the current master:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

print grad(np.linalg.det)(np.eye(3))
```

(It prints an identity matrix.)

But if we just comment out [this `del` statement in grad](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/a811a8226e6ddafb78e37f0553c2d8bdc7af2753/autograd/core.py#L31) it no longer prints an identity matrix and instead prints something like

```
<autograd.numpy.numpy_extra.ArrayNode object at 0x1092184d0>
```

I think the change in behavior could be because the `tapes` member of any `Node` instance is a `WeakKeyDictionary`, so that a `del` statement which removes a strong reference to some `tape` can have side-effects on many `Node.tapes` members. If that's the case, that behavior is pretty confusing, and it might be fragile and/or very Python-implementation-specific (e.g. is WeakKeyDictionary guaranteed to remove entries immediately, or only on garbage collection passes?).

Why does the behavior apparently change when removing that seemingly-critical `del` statement, and is that a good thing?

issue_title: Provide examples of using ServerConfig objects
issue_body:
The API documentation briefly mentions how to effectively use `ServerConfig` objects in the docstring for class `Entity`:

> An entity object is useless if you are unable to use it to communicate with a server. The solution is to provide a nailgun.config.ServerConfig when instantiating a new entity. This configuration object is stored as an instance variable named _server_config and used by methods such as nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity.path().
>
> 1. If the server_config argument is specified, then that is used.
> 2. Otherwise, if nailgun.entity_mixins.DEFAULT_SERVER_CONFIG is set, then that is used.
> 3. Otherwise, call nailgun.config.ServerConfig.get().

That's it, though. And the docstring for `ServerConfig.get` is pretty terrible. The existing API documentation should be fixed up, and a nice example of how to effectively use this capability should be provided.

issue_title: Document the `pluggy._Result` API
issue_body:
Currently [`pluggy._CallOutcome`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy.py#L200) is implied to be part of the internal api by the leading `_`.

In practice it is used by [`hookwrappers`](https://pluggy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#wrappers).

An example:
```python
@pytest.hookimpl(tryfirst=True, hookwrapper=True)
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item, call):
    outcome = yield
    rep = outcome.get_result() 
    ...
```
The `outcome` is accessed directly by the user.
Therefore I propose we expose it correct in `plugg.__all__` and expose it in the [api reference](https://pluggy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html).

issue_title: re-add xml_validation tests
Partition Issues to Prevent Effort Duplication
issue_body:
StakeHolders:

@zancas


Each issue should have an explicit scope of work ideally mentioning modules-packages-files-directories that will be touched.

Once the scope of work is sufficiently well defined someone needs to curate issues to makes sure no 2 issues define the same effort.

If more than 1 issue describes the same effort the duplicate effort needs to be removed.  If all of the effort is duplicated in more than 1 issue then duplicate issues should be closed during the deduplication process.

Tasks For This Issue:
1. read each open issue
    -  If you know information is missing, add information about which parts of the codebase will be touched.
    -  Remember if effort was duplicated in some previous issue and #mention the duplicating issue in this issue, add the "duplicating!" label to this issue and that issue.
2. Once you've performed 1., remove yourself from the "StakeHolders" list, and assign this issue the next StakeHolder.

DO NOT CLOSE ANY ISSUES.   DO NOT SPEND MORE THAN 5 MINUTES ON ANY ONE ISSUE.

issue_title: Templates Are Not Being Overriden
issue_body:
I am trying to embed the Django-Oscar system into a custom HTML template. When I do that, I create a directory `templates/oscar` inside my project's root and place the new template's `base.html` there. However, I am still seeing the Oscar's original template and styles displayed in the browser.

On the other hand, if I place the very same `base.html` directly into the root of the `templates` directory, it is shown, but is missing the static files.

If it helps, here are some settings:

```
# Build paths inside the project like this: os.path.join(BASE_DIR, ...)
BASE_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))


INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',

    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
    'django.contrib.flatpages',
    'django.contrib.sites',
    
    'compressor',
    'widget_tweaks',
] + get_core_apps()

  os.path.join(BASE_DIR, "static"),
]

```

What can I do to fix this? Does Oscar treat templates in some special way? Is this a Django bug? I am using Django 1.11.

issue_title: Kestrel ListenUnixSocket throws `Only ListenType.IPEndPoint is supported` as of 2.1 RC
issue_body:
# Issue
Kestrel ListenUnixSocket throws `Only ListenType.IPEndPoint is supported` as of 2.1 RC

# General

After upgrading to .NET Core 2.1 RC, I am no longer able to use kestrel's 'ListenUnixSocket' feature to use unix sockets instead of TCP sockets.

## Stack Trace
```
Unhandled Exception: System.ArgumentException: Only ListenType.IPEndPoint is supported.
Parameter name: endPointInformation
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Transport.Sockets.SocketTransportFactory.Create(IEndPointInformation endPointInformation, IConnectionDispatcher dispatcher)

--- End of stack trace from previous location where exception was thrown ---

   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Core.ListenOptions.BindAsync(AddressBindContext context)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Core.Internal.AddressBinder.EndpointsStrategy.BindAsync(AddressBindContext context)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Core.Internal.AddressBinder.BindAsync(IServerAddressesFeature addresses, KestrelServerOptions serverOptions, ILogger logger, Func`2 createBinding)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Core.KestrelServer.StartAsync[TContext](IHttpApplication`1 application, CancellationToken cancellationToken)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.Internal.WebHost.StartAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostExtensions.RunAsync(IWebHost host, CancellationToken token, String shutdownMessage)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostExtensions.RunAsync(IWebHost host, CancellationToken token)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostExtensions.Run(IWebHost host)
   at SampleNamespace.Website.Program.Main(String[] args) in C:\BuildAgent.VSTS\_work\26\s\SampleNamespace.Website\Program.cs:line 18
```

## Code
```CSharp
  WebHost.CreateDefaultBuilder(args)
#if DEBUG
                .UseEnvironment("Development")
#elif STAGING
                .UseEnvironment("Staging")
#else
                .UseEnvironment("Production")
                .UseApplicationInsights()
#endif
                .UseStartup<Startup>()
                .UseKestrel(options =>

                    {
                        options.ListenUnixSocket("/tmp/kestrel.sock");

                    //options.Listen(IPAddress.Loopback, 8080);
                })
                .Build();
```

## Extra Info
**OS:** Ubuntu 16.04.4
**.NET Core Version:** 2.1.300-rc1-008673

Would this have anything to do with Kestrel's update in 2.1 where HTTPs is the default while debugging?

issue_title: Json Reference Loop Problem - Possible Solution
issue_body: For a long time the people talk about the reference loop problem when serializing a json, would it not be possible to implement a solution where the referenced objects of the first layer are used as search objects for the lower layers and when it encounters an equal object put a null from that point forward? I know that there are problems with performance and etc. of doing searches to solve a simple problem, is it simple or are there other problems in doing this?

Installer installs x86-64 version on ARMv7 Ubuntu Chromebook
issue_body:
The install-script is buggy.

If I go to:
https://www.microsoft.com/net/core#debian

It says I should do this:
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dotnet/cli/rel/1.0.0-preview1/scripts/obtain/dotnet-install.sh | bash /dev/stdin --version 1.0.0-preview1-002702 --install-dir ~/dotnet

However, when I do that, I get:
(trusty)root@localhost:~# dotnet
bash: /usr/local/bin/dotnet: cannot execute binary file: Exec format error 


Then I run file /root/dotnet/dotnet
and discover
/root/dotnet/dotnet: ELF 64-bit LSB  executable, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked (uses shared libs), for GNU/Linux 2.6.24, BuildID[sha1]=4d1d55536bcbcce0a35a094ae691f3607b8c15a6, not stripped

However, uname -m shows
armv7l

(trusty)root@localhost:~# uname -m
armv7l

And cat /proc/cpuinfo shows  
processor	: 0
model name	: ARMv7 Processor rev 3 (v7l)
Features	: swp half thumb fastmult vfp edsp thumbee neon vfpv3 tls vfpv4 idiva idivt vfpd32 lpae 
Hardware	: NVIDIA Tegra SoC (Flattened Device Tree)
Revision	: 0000


The install script installs the x86-64 version on a ARM 32-bit version. 
BUG, you can't do that. 
Don't use x86 processors anymore, they consume too much power.

issue_title: letsencrypt-auto should not require to git clone entire repo
issue_body:
People are confused that running `letsencrypt-auto` from latest `master` gets them packages from PyPI and not bleeding edge.

Proof of concept for one of the solutions: https://github.com/kuba/letsencrypt/blob/auto.sh/letsencrypt-auto.sh. Thoughts? @pde, @bmw?

issue_title: Upgrade to parsedatetime relase in le-auto
issue_body: While not released yet, bear/parsedatetime#155 caused failures for both `master` and the boulder team today. When the fix is release to PyPI, we should make sure we update `letsencrypt-auto` to use the new version.

issue_title: Is the "n" parameter of jwk handled correctly
issue_body:
I've looked at some PHP libraries that calculate the `n` parameter of jwt, and all generate a value that differs from the one generated by letsencrypt (and accepted by boulder).

Basically, all the libraries generate this parameter in the way described in [this sample script](https://3v4l.org/hUn3t).

issue_title: default for auth -> install
issue_body:
Right now, if auth is followed by install, all the information, e.g. cert_path, is required to be entered again. 
Might make sense to use the 'auth' information as default (or at least offer the option to reuse without re-entering).

issue_title: What unit is Customer.poll's timeout?
issue_body: The `Customer.subscribe()` method takes an optional argument `timeout`. The [documentation](https://docs.confluent.io/current/clients/confluent-kafka-python/index.html#consumer) does not specify the unit of this variable. Is it seconds?

issue_title: how do I specify the path to `matlab`?
issue_body:
it looks like I should be able to specify the keyword argument "excecutable"[sic?] somewhere.

``` python
class MatlabKernel(MetaKernel):

...

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        excecutable = kwargs.pop('excecutable', 'matlab')
        super(MatlabKernel, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self._matlab = Matlab(excecutable)
        self._matlab.start()
```
where is MatlabKernel/MetaKernel getting these kwargs from? can I specify them in the kernel spec or somewhere else?

issue_title: Version 2.5.0 does not parse string that 2.4.2 does
issue_body:
I really like dateutils, because it handles some really ugly corner cases for me, like this one:

```
>>> import dateutil
>>> print dateutil.__version__
2.4.2
>>> from dateutil import parser as date_parser
>>> example_date = '15/08/14,10:52:07-20q'  # what my application gives me -- totally non-standard
>>> date_parser.parse(example_date[:17])
datetime.datetime(2014, 8, 15, 10, 52, 7)
```

Or at least, it did until version 2.5.0:

```
>>> import dateutil
>>> print dateutil.__version__
2.5.0
>>> from dateutil import parser as date_parser
>>> example_date = '15/08/14,10:52:07-20q'
>>> date_parser.parse(example_date[:17])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateutil/parser.py", line 1161, in parse
    return DEFAULTPARSER.parse(timestr, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateutil/parser.py", line 552, in parse
    raise ValueError("Unknown string format")
ValueError: Unknown string format
```
I hope this is easy to fix.

issue_title: Centering and Auto-zooming Choropleth maps
issue_body:
Is there a recognised way of centering a choropleth map based on a bounding box around the extent of boundaries included in a geoJSON file?

I didn't have time to look around to see if there is a polished boundary box detector routine anywhere, so I used the following quick script:

    import json
    jf=open(GEOJSON_FILE,'r').read()
    jj=json.loads(jf)
    
    acc={'minlat':999,'maxlat':-999,'minlon':999,'maxlon':-999}
    for feature in jj['features']:
        for c in feature['geometry']['coordinates']:
            for cc in c:
            #My testfile had lest of co-ords in co-ord list? Should check them rather than ignore them..
                if type(cc[0]) is list:break
                if acc['maxlat']<cc[1]: acc['maxlat']=cc[1]
                if acc['maxlon']<cc[0]: acc['maxlon']=cc[0]
                if acc['minlat']<cc[1]: acc['minlat']=cc[1]
                if acc['minlon']<cc[0]: acc['minlon']=cc[0]
    centre_lat=acc['minlat']+(acc['maxlat']-acc['minlat'])/2
    centre_lon=acc['minlon']+(acc['maxlon']-acc['minlon'])/2

I'm not sure how to set the zoom though? A heuristic based on the height/width of the bounding box?

2. They each have `ndwi`, `ndwi1`, and `ndwi2`, but `ndwi` and `ndwi1` are identical - they both have the same input (`['nir', 'swir1']`). I'm guessing that this is because users may have interest in accessing a general `ndwi` band without having to specify the SWIR input, but it would help if this was documented. 
3. There's an error in the `description` for all of the `ndwi2` bands, as they all take in `['nir', 'swir2']`, but their descriptions all say they use SWIR1: `'Normalized Difference Water Index (with SWIR1)'`.
4. Are the panchromatic bands not being included for the Landsat satellites?
5. It would be really helpful if the native bands included the band number in their label, e.g., for L8, `'band2_blue'` instead of just `'blue'`.  
6. The band names are returned in alphabetical order, but I found that confusing, particularly with how the native bands were mixed in with the derived bands. I think it would be more useful if the native bands were returned first, in proper band order, followed by the derived bands.
7. Since the first Landsat 8 band is technically 'Ultrablue (coastal/aerosol)', it might be helpful to have that included in the name; it's currently just `'aerosol'`. 
8. I think having the wavelength range included in the metadata for the native bands would be helpful as well.

issue_title: Integrate Glances
issue_body:
A new metric could be to show any metric marked as critical by [Glances](https://nicolargo.github.io/glances/).

Here are Glances levels:

 * GREEN: OK (everything is fine)
 * BLUE: CAREFUL (need attention)
 * VIOLET: WARNING (alert)
 * RED: CRITICAL (critical)

For example if the "CPU ctx_sw" and "MEM used" are marked as CRITICAL, then show in the powerline status bar in RED: `CPU ctx_sw, MEM used` or `CPY ctx_sw: 16000 - MEM used: 17.3G`.

Ideally clicking it (with mouse mode on), would open Glances.

This has advantages over most other metrics in that I'm usually interested to see if something is possibly wrong and it may be one of many many metrics/sensors. Glances does monitor a lot of metrics including but not limited to:

 * CPU usage, iowait, context switches...
 * MEMORY used
 * SWAP used
 * NETWORK I/O
 * DISK I/O and free space
 * SENSORS like fans and temperature of the PC
 * DOCKER containers

It has at the bottom a "critical alert detected" list which may be easy to capture and reduce to a few characters if there is any issue detected.

Ideally one may want to also get alerts for any level with highest alert first and using colors like Glances.

issue_title: schema2parameters - body + header
issue_body:
Hey,

I have a schema and the default_in = body, but one of the fields has a headers location.
schema2parameters inserts the header field inside the body instead of returning one body param and one header param.

What do I do?

``` python
class AccountsSchema(Schema):
    user_id = fields.Int(required=True, location="headers")
    name = fields.Str(required=True, validate=validate.Length(min=1, max=255), location="json")
    status = fields.Str(required=True, validate=validate.OneOf(choices=["Active", "Paused"]), location="json")
```

``` python
def fields2parameters(fields, schema=None, spec=None, use_refs=True, dump=True,
                      default_in='body', name='body', required=False):
    """Return an array of OpenAPI parameters given a mapping between field names and
    :class:`Field <marshmallow.Field>` objects. If `default_in` is "body", then return an array
    of a single parameter; else return an array of a parameter for each included field in
    the :class:`Schema <marshmallow.Schema>`.

    https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#parameterObject
    """
    if default_in == 'body':
        if schema is not None:
            # Prevent circular import
            from apispec.ext.marshmallow import resolve_schema_dict
            prop = resolve_schema_dict(spec, schema, dump=dump)
        else:
            prop = fields2jsonschema(fields, spec=spec, use_refs=use_refs, dump=dump)

        return [{
            'in': default_in,
            'required': required,
            'name': name,
            'schema': prop,
        }]

    assert not getattr(schema, 'many', False), \
        "Schemas with many=True are only supported for 'json' location (aka 'in: body')"

    exclude_fields = getattr(getattr(schema, 'Meta', None), 'exclude', [])

    return [
        field2parameter(
            field_obj,
            name=_observed_name(field_obj, field_name),
            spec=spec,
            use_refs=use_refs,
            dump=dump,
            default_in=default_in
        )
            for field_name, field_obj in iteritems(fields)
            if (
                (field_name not in exclude_fields)
                and not (field_obj.dump_only and not dump)
            )
    ]
```

issue_title: Error on serialization of custom class - if attr is callable it is assumed its constructor takes no arguments
issue_body:
The issue got introduced in 2.0.0b5 by the following diff and is still present in 2.7.1:

```
--- marshmallow-2.0.0b4/utils.py        2016-04-12 18:04:58.209260000 -0600
+++ marshmallow-2.0.0b5/utils.py        2016-04-12 18:06:52.287353000 -0600
@@ -316,7 +316,8 @@
         return obj[key]
     except (KeyError, AttributeError, IndexError, TypeError):
         try:
-            return getattr(obj, key)
+            attr = getattr(obj, key)
+            return attr() if callable(attr) else attr
         except AttributeError:
             return default
     return default
```

While trying to traverse and hence serialize certain objects, it might encounter an object which can't be indexed, calls the exception handler which tests it being callable. If so, it does.

issue_title: dump doesn't validate @validates_schema
issue_body:
It's pretty surprising that there's no way to dump + validates schema as expected, here's a setup:

```
class Scheme(Schema):
    num = fields.Integer(default=1)
    num2 = fields.Integer(default=2)
    @validates_schema
    def _valid(self, data):
        if not 'num' in data and not 'num2' in data:
            raise ValidationError('Either num or num2 must be supplied')

Invalid Data:
>>> data = {}
>>> schema = Scheme()
>>> schema.dump(data)
MarshalResult(data={'num2': 2, 'num': 1}, errors={})
>>> schema.validate(data)
{'_schema': ['Either num or num2 must be supplied']}
>>> schema.load(data)
UnmarshalResult(data={}, errors={'_schema': ['Either num or num2 must be supplied']})


Valid Data:
>>> schema.dump(data)
MarshalResult(data={'num2': 2, 'num': 1}, errors={})
>>> schema.validate(data)
{}
>>> schema.load(data)
UnmarshalResult(data={'num': 1}, errors={})
```


None of these methods validate both fields + validates_schema and also apply default values (through marshalling).

issue_title: Loompy file export for Scanpy
issue_body:
Dear Linnarsson Lab,
do you think it would make sense to build a loom file export into [Scanpy](http://scanpy.readthedocs.io)? I haven't thought too much about the file format for Scanpy's [AnnData class](https://scanpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/scanpy.api.AnnData.html), which follows similar ideas as compared to the loom file format. Even though, in principle, it's file format achieves similar things than the loom file format - maybe I didn't get everything - we would really like to use some more widely accepted platform-/language-independent way of storing AnnData objects. The scater/scran developers are backing on a hdf5-format for SingleCellExperiment, so we thought about adapting this as it's probably used by many people...
Maybe we could have a brief Skype for discussing this? I'm presenting Scanpy at Broad in two weeks and it would be nice to have a good solution for the file format until then.
Cheers,
  Alex
PS: AnnData will be implemented in a cleaner way, soon.

issue_title: rusage.contents could contain multiple equals signs
issue_body:
In helpers.py:
````python
    def adapt_rusage(rusage)
````

It splits the attributes in rusage.contents with:
````python
   k, v = attr.split('=')
````
On my system (PBS Pro 12.2 and pbs-drmma library) there is the possibility of an attribute containing an equals signs so it requires:
````python
    k, v = attr.split('=',1)
````

Otherwise it does not cleanly end the job and causes problems in software such as Galaxy.
Happy to provide patch.

issue_title: Improve support for arbitrary license names
issue_body:
The current implementation has an arbitrary limitation that an expression with `and`, `or` or `with` keywords that are part of a license name and not part a keyword of the expression would not parse nor resolve correctly.

Now this limitation does not need to exist as when a list of LicenseRef is provided that is otherwise not ambiguous (e.g. with no name resolving to more than one license key) the parsing is not ambiguous.

So I propose to update this to handle properly expressions with such licenses. This would work only when resolution on parsing is requested or when resolution is requested AND that license references are provided.

issue_title: simplify the clearsky.ineichen function
issue_body:
I think that clearsky.ineichen is a disaster of a function. There's some unnecessary complexity inherited from PVLIB Matlab, and some additional complexity that I wrongly added.

In the spirit of simple functions, I think we should:
* remove the ``time, latitude, location`` arguments in favor of ``apparent_zenith``
* make ``airmass`` a required argument
* remove the ability for the function to calculate it's own solar position and airmass
* remove the ability for the function to look up linke turbidity

The ``Location`` class's ``get_clearsky`` method, or similar, can provide the higher-level features.

My simplified solis PR #148 might provide some inspiration.

issue_title: fix numkit float/int indexing
issue_body:
The following numkit tests in `test_integration.py` fail
* Test_simps_error.test_odd_const_x 
* Test_simps_error.test_odd_const_x_dx

The problem is in `numkit.integration.simps_error()`:
```
            if len(x.shape) == 1:
                shapex = numpy.ones(nd)
                shapex[axis] = x.shape[0]
                saveshape = x.shape
                returnshape = True
               x=x.reshape(tuple(shapex))

E               TypeError: 'numpy.float64' object cannot be interpreted as an index
```
The argument for reshap (`shapex`) must be an integer or tuple of integers. 

# Fix
Probably just
```python
                shapex = numpy.ones(nd, dtype=np.int_)
```

# Background
* see https://github.com/Becksteinlab/GromacsWrapper/pull/110#issuecomment-288475330 for initial discussion and e.g. https://travis-ci.org/Becksteinlab/GromacsWrapper/jobs/187547479 for failure in numkit `test_integration`
* see rbgirshick/py-faster-rcnn#481 for description: deprecated indexing with floats has been removed in numpy 1.12.

issue_title: Mutagen writes, but won't read, multiple identical front cover images in MP3 files
issue_body:
Mutagen allowed me to write two identical front cover images, so now the files have two identical APIC tags. However, mutagen will only read one of them, nor will it tell me there are multiple front cover images so I could handle the issue.  mid3v2 only lists one APIC tag as well, while id3v2 lists both tags, and easyTAG also shows both front cover images.


`$ mid3v2 -l ../music/mp3/Queen/Greatest\ Hits\ I\ \&\ II/Queen\ -\ Under\ Pressure.mp3 
IDv2 tag info for ../music/mp3/Queen/Greatest Hits I & II/Queen - Under Pressure.mp3
APIC=cover front, Front cover (image/jpeg, 65082 bytes)
COMM=description=XXX=<rating>6</rating>
TALB=Greatest Hits I & II
`

`$ id3v2 -l ../music/mp3/Queen/Greatest\ Hits\ I\ \&\ II/Queen\ -\ Under\ Pressure.mp3 
id3v2 tag info for ../music/mp3/Queen/Greatest Hits I & II/Queen - Under Pressure.mp3:
TCON (Content type): Classic Rock (1)
APIC (Attached picture): (Front cover)[, 3]: image/jpeg, 65082 bytes
APIC (Attached picture): (Front cover)[, 3]: image/jpeg, 65082 bytes
COMM (Comments): (description)[XXX]: <rating>6</rating>
`

Both
`            d = mutagen.mp3.MP3(self.mp3Path)
            x = d.tags.getall('APIC')`
and
`self.apic = self.m.getall('APIC')`
where m is: `self.m = mutagen.id3.ID3(self.mp3Path, None, False, 3)`
only return one image, when a list of apic objects is expected.

It'd be nice if mutagen didn't write off-spec files, and it'd also be nice if mutagen would read off-spec apic tags so you could detect the off-spec condition and fix it.

issue_title: fp.roundToIntegral issue
issue_body:
For the following benchmark:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint 8 24))
(assert (= s0 (fp.roundToIntegral roundNearestTiesToEven ((_ to_fp 8 24) roundNearestTiesToEven (/ 9 10)))))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
```

Z3 responds:

```
sat
((s0 (_ +zero 8 24)))
```

i.e., ` +0` as the nearest integral value to `0.9` as a single-precision float. I think the correct answer should be `+1`.

issue_title: Re-consider finalizers usage in Z3 Java API: now with some hard data
issue_body:
Hi,

I have tried to open a ticket about this before with little success, I'll try again now, with some more evidence.
Z3 Java API relies on finalizers to clean up memory.
There exist numerous books and articles, starting with "Effective Java", stating that finalizers are not a good replacement for C++ destructors, and should generally be avoided.
The main problem is that the object can be "resurrected" in the finalizer, and the GC needs to do a second run ensuring that that hasn't happened.
Just adding an empty `finalize` method to an often used object can drastically decrease the performance of an application.

Conceptually, there is no reason to use finalizers in place of PhantomReferences: everything which can be done with finalizers, can be done with PhantomReferences, but faster.
Moreover, with PhantomReferences it is very easy to switch tracking on and off on demand, for example with a command line flag.

In our JavaSMT library we support both our own Java API, generated using JNI, and the one provided by Z3. The code in two APIs is very similar, the main difference lies in the memory management.
We would really like to switch from our home-grown Z3 JNI API to the Z3-supplied one, but we can't due to the heavy performance penalty associated with finalizers usage.

In our API, we use `PhantomReference`s to call `dec_ref` on Z3 ASTs and Models, and we use the try-with-resources syntax for opening and closing contexts (as normally context objects have a single owner).

Finally, I evaluate the performance of my verification tool in 3 different modes:

 - Not calling dec_refs at all, simply leaking memory (Our JNI API)
 - Using PhantomReference, cleaning up the queue after every allocation (Our JNI API)
 - Using finalizers (Z3 Java API)

This is the evaluation table: http://metaworld.me/finalizers_results.html
The table is generated with the benchexec tool, which takes resource usage very seriously (cf. https://github.com/sosy-lab/benchexec for description and the paper).

As can be seen, ironically, the best configuration by far is not tracking the memory at all, and just leaking it.
Of course that is also due to the fact that the programs I'm analyzing here are quite small, and the results could be different for a different dataset.

However, the table clearly shows that switching from phantom references to finalizers gives a huge performance hit (CPU time nearly doubles for correctly solved instances) and it more then doubles the memory usage!

Based on this evidence I think it is pretty clear that finalizers are not an optimal solution for memory management.

issue_title: Refactor seems to have caused issues when files exist but in the wrong format
issue_body:
On my system, angr-doc tests fail because they attempt to load an arm binary, depending on libc.so. My machine has `/usr/arm-linux-gnueabi/lib/libc.so`, which CLE finds and attempt so load, but it can't be loaded since it's an ldscript, not an actual elf. When `identify_object` fails to find the backend, it raises a `CLECompatibilityError`, which is never caught and causes the program to halt.

The correct behavior is that the loader continues to search for a valid libc.so, and if none is found, the dependency goes unresolved (contingent on the value of `except_missing_libs`)

I can't assign you for some reason, but this is the job of @subwire.

issue_title: SSH unit tests fail due to paramiko changes removing try/except around SSH transport timeout
issue_body:
The following changed removed a catch of AttributeError which was being caught/passed by the unit tests in ncclient.

https://github.com/paramiko/paramiko/commit/cdb2326612985b3969d2c2064eba8d16146463f8

They removed the try/except block and just left the timeout setting code. The ncclient unit tests were following a pattern where None was being passed into the paramiko.Transport __init__ as the socket object. Since you can't set a timeout on None, we now see AttributeError coming back through the unit tests and everything's been failing since roughly March of last year.

We should either catch AttributeError in the unit tests or remove the step of setting the timeout.

issue_title: [feature-request] Volume normalization while streaming
issue_body:
It would be nice to have a consistent volume provide by the streamer. Since youtube does not normalize volume, and there are libre-source libraries which can normalize volume/loudness on the fly, it would be nice to have the feature integrated into mps streamer.

https://askubuntu.com/questions/95716/automatically-adjust-the-volume-based-on-content
https://apps.ubuntu.com/cat/applications/swh-plugins/

Actually...this may be a bit much to ask of the streaming app, although it has priveledged information about the start point of new songs.


This may be difficult to do while streaming, especially since it has to be done on the fly without interfering with intended usage.  However it may not be possible to do it adequately without downloading the whole song.

There's probably some reasonably small beginning fraction of the song which has a maximum loudness similar to the max loudness of the song as a whole, not sure what that is. This request is probably too nebulous, but the feature may even be useful deal with songs having sudden spikes in volume.

issue_title: Hidden schema property shouldn't cause problems behind a proxy
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'm seeing the situation where I would like to hide a schema attribute behind a proxy, specifically 'is_prepared'. (See #29)

If I hide a schema attribute I naively expect it to be hidden at the proxy level but available again when I perform ```myStrippedObject = stripProxy(myObject)```.

The problem I'm seeing is that the attribute is not assigned (possibly just in the default case I'm not sure) when the attribute is hidden and I think this is due to a problem with what I expect vs what is implemented in ```GangaObjects.py```

I would expect that all attributes even hidden ones be set to the default values behind the scenes in the Objects and that the only 'hiding' that is done is when the Proxy advertises the new dictionary to the GPI.

This requires a few lines of change in the GangaObject and a bit of work in the Proxy but does anyone see any possible problems with working this way rather than what is currently done?

At the moment I see ```is_prepared``` in ```myStrippedObject.__class__.__dict__``` as well as in the schema but I don't see it as an attribute and ```hasattr(myStrippedObject, 'is_prepared')``` will fail which is not what I'm naively expecting. (Naturally the hidden attribute is not available through ```myObject``` either by design).
Or am I missing something with how hidden attributes are hidden?

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: Dirac/Ganga job statuses
issue_body:
Hi all,

From memory there is a good reason about having the ```"Done"``` and ```"Complete"``` status in Dirac marked as ```completed``` in ganga.
(I think this was something to do with data replication etc...)

However, when I run my branch #430 I'm seeing jobs consistently being marked as failed as the job is not getting the output file until some time after the job has finished.

After digging around I found my jobs were passing through the monitoring cycle too quickly and were completing whilst Dirac was still processing the output from the Job.

This is responsible for at least some of the problems of jobs completing ahead of the output sandbox or data being made available.

In interests of getting something working I'm keen to move to have only Dirac jobs which are in a ```"Done"``` state (in DIRAC) marked ```completed``` (in Ganga).

Alternatively I'm going to move to mark all jobs in the ```"Complete"``` state but expect some output data in the form of a DiracFile (aka. asking Dirac to upload some output for us) as being in the running state. This is the only way to get Ganga to wait for the output data to be made available as requesting it before it's been uploaded will _work_ and will get an empty output data set which has been a pain to debug as the data exists in DIRAC and could be manually requested in Ganga but was missing after the monitoring loop progressed a job to completed.

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: trailing_slash_redirect prevent from creating views with a trailing slash in plugins.
issue_body:
Serving assets with pyramid default the subpath to ``index.html`` in case a trailing slash is present.

With ``trailing_slash_redirect`` the redirection happens before reaching the static route defined in the plugin.
We should change the trailing_slash_redirect to catch a 404 and redirect if there is a trailing slash only in that case rather than defining a catchall route that redirects any url even existing ones.

Refs: https://github.com/Kinto/kinto-dist/pull/3

issue_title: kinto.id_generator option doesn't work
issue_body:
#### Issue
As per the [docs](http://kinto.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/settings.html?highlight=id_generator#feature-settings), we should be able to define a `kinto.id_generator` options that will work for records, but this is not the case in the current (3.4.0.dev0) implementation.

#### Easy workaround
As an easy fix, you can specify the `record` type as in `kinto.record_id_generator`.

issue_title: increased latency with multi camera in the same process
issue_body:
We're using RS415 with FW 5.8.14.0. librealsense 2.8.3, Windows 10 1709.
When using one RS415 we're seeing low latency, but when we connect 3 cameras we see a very obvious increase in latency. We can reproduce the issue in the multi-cam example app.
I'm attaching links to 2 videos showing the issue:
one camera: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1eTAxBEujdxrIbn6UgkqmwUVRpPiCoJNx
three cameras: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1Aqve0U4FBlgJPOMnHyQRbiwyz5tRMMor

We also ran an experiment where we run each camera in a separate process at the same time, and the latency was low, just like in one cameras case.
We have a dedicated USB controller per cameras.

We are seeing increased latency with DS430 when running 3 in parallel as well.

issue_title: Update readArgs() func so works for all parameters
dt is not properly set when using h.stdinit() aot h.init()
issue_body:
When dt!=0.025, the value for dt set with h.stdinit() doesn't get properly set. Change was made at:

https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/commit/db64b4b495b23788ec07d1e1a06b4b2bfd82bfd4#diff-a52fad7191c089fb6b67f5f3c6d75043L991

Try setting dt=0.01 in https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/master/examples/HHTut/HHTut.py to see this.

I suspect this is down to not setting steps_per_ms (or other variables) in addition to dt when it's changed...

issue_title: Version on pip is outdated, docs are confusing
issue_body:
There has apparently been a major rework of the interface here, which looks good overall.  Unfortunately, the suggestion to use "pip install git-pw" is, at the moment, very bad advice:
- You get the 0.2.0 version interface
- The docs point you to the new interface

Very confusing.  Suggest making a new release and uploading it to pip soon if you can; or at least taking the suggestion to use pip out of the README.

issue_title: When resolvers return a type that implements an interface, queries against that field return `null` unless the type has been used elsewhere on the `Query` class.
http://swapi.graphene-python.org/ leads to 502
Invalid Keyword Argument when using Enum
Does relay.NodeField support many args more than "id" ?
issue_body:
Hi, guys

In `graphene/relay/fields.py` : 

```python
class NodeField(Field):
    # ...
    def id_fetcher(self, global_id, info):
        # ...
        return object_type.get_node(_id, info)

    def resolver(self, instance, args, info):
        global_id = args.get('id')
        return self.id_fetcher(global_id, info)
```

This define a `get_node` strictly, just global id could be used as arg. 

There is an example about other scene: 

[http://graphql-swapi.parseapp.com](http://graphql-swapi.parseapp.com/?query={%0A%20%20film%28filmID%3A%20%223%22%29%20{%0A%20%20%20%20id%0A%20%20%20%20title%0A%20%20}%0A%20%20allFilms%20{%0A%20%20%20%20edges%20{%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20node%20{%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20id%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20title%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20}%0A%20%20%20%20}%0A%20%20}%0A}%0A)

They define a more useful interface: 

```
film(id: ID, filmID: ID): Film
person(id: ID, personID: ID): Person
planet(id: ID, planetID: ID): Planet
species(id: ID, speciesID: ID): Species
starship(id: ID, starshipID: ID): Starship
vehicle(id: ID, vehicleID: ID): Vehicle
node(id: ID!): Node
```

It has a 'XXXID' arg in every node field. This is just what I wanted. It would be useful where a `NodeField` can queried by other args not just id.

Any help would be appreciated.
Thanks!

Refs:

- [Relay Global Object Identification Specification](https://facebook.github.io/relay/graphql/objectidentification.htm)

issue_title: Define and use a Run data model
issue_body:
# Overview
"Runs" are one of the fundamental units of data organization in TensorBoard. They are the primary way of compartmentalizing data across different invocations of TensorFlow and diffing the results in the UI.

Surprisingly, given their privileged role in the system, they have no explicit data model. Instead, runs are defined only by their location on disk, and the only metadata available is the directory name. Consequently, managing TensorBoards with many different runs is quite cumbersome. 

As we move into the TensorBase/SQL database world, we can reasonably expect both that the number of runs available will dramatically increase (increasing the need for metadata and organization), and that location in the filesystem will stop being a useful organizational system.

I'll examine a few very common TB workflows, and show how they are handicapped by the lack of run metadata, and could be improved.

## Iterative Development
### The Workflow
I'm iterating on a model. Every time I tweak it, I re-run my model with new parameters (maybe once every minute or two with small models).

### Current Approaches
If I do this naïvely, then the output from my many "runs" will all wind up in a single directory (defined by re-creating the FileWriter with a fixed string); thus, the runs will be mangled together into one "run" that inappropriately concatenates the output from many TF runs. TensorBoard handles this badly, and will show "triangle charts" that finish, return to origin, and start again.

One less-naïve approach would be to write custom code for a FileWriter that auto-increments directory name, so I would wind up with "run1", "run2", "run3", etc. In practice, this would quickly lead to a very noisy TensorBoard in which the salient data (performance on my latest run) is obscured by the results from all my previous runs.

In practice, I simply delete all of my log data before each run, sidestepping the TB run model entirely. (In practice, this seems to occasionally break TB, so I need to restart it every few runs.)

### Better Approaches
Suppose we had the following three pieces of metadata for each run:
1. The time the run started
2. How successful the run was (i.e. a particular scalar summary is the key metric)
3. (ambitious) The code that generated the run

Then, we could have TensorBoard keep every run in the UI, but only show me my latest two runs, and my best run ever. Then I can glance at TB and immediately see how my new run is faring compared to my last approach, and my historical best. This would be particularly great on the scalar dashboard, since it would let me compare (for slow jobs) how good my run is at its latest step compared to my best ever run at the same step.

If we kept the code that generated the run, I could even do a code diff to remember what my approach was back when I had my most successful run. This would make TB into a much more effective "lab notebook" / "experiment journal". (On the same note, it would be awesome if I could add written annotations to each run.)

For this approach to work, it's assumed that every time the FileWriter/Run is initialized, it writes out a signature in the event log, so that we can distinguish between the last event of run `n-1` and the first event of run `n`.

## Hyperparameter Optimization
### The Workflow
I have a model, and it's time to tune it via hyperparameter search. I define (say) 5 hps to vary, and 5 different values for each hp, resulting in 25 runs. I want to see how these different sets of HPs perform, with a particular emphasis on looking at the combinations that got the best performance.

### Current Approaches
I create a "hyperparameter string" for each run, e.g. "lr=1e-2,beta=0.5,n_fc=3,n_conv=4,act=relu". These strings need to fit in a small space in the UI, so interpretability is sacrificed. My only structured way to search across these approaches (e.g. to see how the class of models with learning rate 1e-2 and 4 convolutional layers performed) is to write regexes like "lr=1e-2.*n_conv=4". If my experiment generated a large number of runs, merely discovering which one performed best on my metrics may be a chore.

Here's an example (note: dozens of runs below this not shown)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1400023/30949552-f951c80c-a3cb-11e7-9f5f-a709b66a13c6.png)



### Better Approaches
We should add explicit support for hyperparameters with a flexible data model that includes:
* support for different kinds of hparams (binary, float val, integer val)
* provenance for hparams, since they may have come from intermediate tools/frameworks and be tricky to hunt down
* longform descriptions for hparams, so you can write down what they are

Then we can make a UI that has explicit support for this information, like:
* providing a [parallel coordinate plot](https://bl.ocks.org/jasondavies/1341281) that allows easy visualization of how different hparams affected performance
* surfacing the top-K runs according to a metric for explicit comparison
* UI affordance for filtering run selection by hyperparameters (perhaps drag on the parallel coordinates plot)

## Train/Test/Eval Comparison
### The Workflow
Within each conceptual "run" as described above, we may record TF metrics on the training data, and then, at the end of the run, separately on a test set of data. The metric we care about may be performance on the test set rather than the train data.

### Current Apporaches
It's common to record separate runs for test and train by appending a "/train" or "/eval" suffix on the run name. Since TensorBoard is not aware that there is a relationship between "foo/train" and "foo/eval" but not between "foo/train" and "bar/eval", it can be very hard to filter them or compare them using the UI. The same example from above demonstrates this problem:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1400023/30949552-f951c80c-a3cb-11e7-9f5f-a709b66a13c6.png)

### Better Approaches
Since this is both very common and fundamental to machine learning, I think we should privilege it in the data model. Then, we can use the information to make more intelligent displays on the scalar dashboard, e.g. drawing all the eval losses and train losses on separate graphs, and using consistent color motifs for the train and eval portions of runs.


## Run organization
In addition to adding data that describes individual runs, I think we should add some higher level organization between runs. Especially since we expect to have organization-level TensorBoards that may serve dozens of users, each of whom have hundreds or thousands of runs!

A very simple system would be to have Projects as a top-level construct that defines boundaries of comparability. Two runs in different projects will never be relevant to compare, e.g. comparing an image model to a text model is meaningless. Within projects, experiments will contain tightly related runs, e.g. all the different runs for a given hyperparameter search.

However, it might be better to not make hard assumptions about the way that researchers will want to organize data, and instead come up with a simple abstraction they can apply to their purposes, e.g. a directory structure abstraction.

We should also add information like:
* owner
* group (esp. relevant for visibility restriction)

It might also be helpful to add "search terms" like "image", "mnist", "cifar", "gan", etc. This would assist people doing meta-analyses of research efforts, or looking to explore for successful approaches to related problems.

## Pitfalls
We need to make sure we can distinguish "started a new run" from "restarted a previous run from checkpoint" (we should be able to depend on the [SessionLog proto](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/util/event.proto#L64)).

## Field ideas
* Creation time
* Type (train/test/eval)
* Key metric id (/ performance of key metric?)
* Author
* Group
* ProjectId?
* Hyperparameters (likely use Tensor encoding for each HParam, to accomodate generic types)
* Longform description
* Annotations (generated via TB UI)
* Star (maybe useful so we can signal to TB to keep this run rather than garbage collecting - e.g. star just the best performing run in a hyperparameter search)
* UUID (useful for keeping in a large run DB)


## Next Steps
Whoever takes this on (@wchargin?) should define a data model for runs that accomodates these use cases, and a corresponding API that lets users populate that data model, along with a sketch of how the new data will be used in the UI. Once we agree on the data model, come up with and execute a plan that gets these features into TensorBoard :) 

We don't need to add everything at once. An incremental approach would be fine.

cc @jart @chihuahua @nfelt

issue_title: Tensorboard cuts off top of graphs
issue_body: Consider Stack Overflow for getting support using TensorBoard - they have a larger community with better searchability: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/tensorboard For bug reports, please include the following: - TensorBoard version (from pip package, also printed out when running `tensorboard`) tensorboard 1.8.0 - TensorFlow version if different from TensorBoard tensorflow 1.8.0 - OS Platform and version (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04) ``` ❯ lsb_release -a No LSB modules are available. Distributor ID: Ubuntu Description: Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS Release: 16.04 Codename: xenial ``` - Python version (e.g. 2.7, 3.5) ``` ❯ python -V Python 3.6.5 ``` - For browser-related issues: - Browser type and version (e.g. Chrome 64.0.3282.140) Chrome Version 68.0.3440.84 (Official Build) (64-bit) - Screenshot if it's a visual issue Please describe the bug as clearly as possible, and if possible provide a minimal example (code, data, and/or command line) to reproduce the issue. Thanks! This is a minor issue, but tensorflow has a tendency to cut off the tops of peaking graph lines after clicking the "Fit domain to data" button: Of course, these are the lines I usually care the most about. I find myself constantly setting smoothing to .9, clicking the "Fit domain to data" button, and then setting smoothing back to .99. Is this avoidable somehow?

issue_title: otThreadSetMasterkey
issue_body:
The api call is given by:
```
ThreadError otThreadSetMasterKey(otInstance *aInstance, const uint8_t *aKey, uint8_t aKeyLength)
```
If this is called with a masterkey that is not 16 bytes in length, e.g.
```
uint8_t key = {0x5E, 0xA1, 0xED};
otThreadSetMasterKey(aInstance, const &key, 3);
```
The 16 byte masterkey buffer will look like
```
0x5E, 0xA1, 0xED, 0x33, 0x44, 0x55, 0x66, 0x77, 0x88, 0x99, 0xAA, 0xBB, 0xCC, 0xDD, 0xEE, 0xFF
```
because of the default masterkey that was used before. (One could consider clearing the buffer first)

The above results in the following
1. The actual masterkey used is: `0x5E, 0xA1, 0xED, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00`
2. Getting the masterkey results in `0x5E, 0xA1, 0xED, 0x33, 0x44, 0x55, 0x66, 0x77, 0x88, 0x99, 0xAA, 0xBB, 0xCC, 0xDD, 0xEE, 0xFF` with length 16

If another thread devices joins, it will get the entire 16 byte buffer as masterkey. Since Thread does not provide a mechanic to communicate the masterkey's length to the new device, the joiner router and the joiner will have a different master key.

There are two issues:
1. The length field does not seem to be taken into account consistently (code problem)
2. The length field cannot be communicated to a new joiner device (api flaw)

issue_title: Json preview is not clear
Rename and refactor CallSettings
issue_body:
Currently we have CallSettings and CallOptions, and it's hard to say their roles and the differences.

Actually CallSettings is the settings (or configurations) for an API call (or a method), such as the page descriptors or bundling descriptors, retry parameters.

On the other hand, CallOptions is the optional data to modify the behavior of individual invocation of the methods.

To me,
- CallSettings isn't a very clear name. 'Call-' prefix sounds like per-call (per-invocation) thing. Maybe, `MethodSettings`?
- the 'merge' method to create a new call settings doesn't look a good design after the redesign of create_api_call.  I feel like CallSettings (or MethodSettings) should hold the default CallOptions instance, and CallOptions instance should have the `merge()` method to create the actual options for the invocation.

Thoughts?

issue_title: `name` parameter is not used on first plotted line
issue_body:
When plotting a line with the ``vis.line()`` function, one can specify a ``name`` for a trace.
This works fine when `` 'appending' `` new lines, but does not work for the first line you plot in a window.

One can work around the bug by adding a ``legend`` entry to the options, but this is a dirty hack imo...
The reason this is irritating is because this does not allow for a consistent API for updating/removing traces.

# Reproducing bug
```python
import visdom
import numpy as np

vis = visdom.Visdom()
vis.line(np.array([1,2,3]), np.array([0,1,2]), win='my_window', name='custom_trace', opts={'showlegend':True})
# The line will have '1' as name in stead of 'custom_trace'
```

issue_title: Seamlessly conversion nullableString (publicly inherited from std::string) to Python and back.
issue_body:
## Issue description

I have a class which inherits directly from the std::string class but also has the concept of 'null' versus empty (""). This is an important distinction because of it understanding "on the wire" IPC traffic which has different and important distinctions between null and "".

When a method is invoked that returns my class nullableString, Python complains with the following error:
what():  TypeError: Unable to convert function return value to a Python type! The signature was
    (self: Module.GetReply) -> nullableString

I've looked at the documentation and include custom type_casters but it's not clear how to use this to handle the conversion. I am looking at potentially using a custom type caster with pybind11, however, I'm hoping to find a simpler way since the only difference between my class and string is the concept of a null string. All regular methods (c_str(), begin(), end() etc.) are directly accessible to call.

I can add reproducible code, however, it is likely to be less succinct than this explanation.

Thanks,
Salah

issue_title: TypeError when working with object across modules
issue_body:
When a type `MyType` is wrapped in one module and subsequently used as an argument for a function in a different module a TypeError is raised.

Specifically the attached basic test gives the following output:

    In [1]: import amod
    
    In [2]: a=amod.A()
    
    In [3]: mt=amod.MyType()
    
    In [4]: a.test(mt)
    Out[4]: 0L
    
    In [5]: import bmod
    
    In [6]: b=bmod.B()
    
    In [7]: b.test(mt)
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
    <ipython-input-7-7aa8288574de> in <module>()
    ----> 1 b.test(mt)
    
    TypeError: Incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
        1. (bmod.B, std::__1::shared_ptr<mynamespace::MyType>) -> int

[multitest.tar.gz](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/209354/multitest.tar.gz)

Checking where this exception is created and specifically checking the content of `registered_types_cpp` does indicate the correct type `MyType` is visible from `bmod`.

issue_title: keep_alive<0,1>() fails for method where the return value may sometimes validly be nullptr
issue_body:
If keep_alive<0,1> is used a RuntimeError("Could not allocate weak reference!") exception is thrown when nullptr is returned. If keep_alive<0,1> is not used we have a use-after-free bug, and possible segfault.

Minimal example:
[example-341.py.txt](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/421455/example-341.py.txt)
[example-341.cpp.txt](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/421456/example-341.cpp.txt)

This problem is fixed by https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/341

issue_title: CMake builds create a module with missing init function
issue_body:
The CMake example results in a broken module (when adding extra libraries). This is split of from #387 since I suspect there is a larger issue than just lack of documentation.

I am trying to create a module that wraps a C++ library, using CMake.  My first attempt based on #387 was to use target_link_libraries with PRIVATE. This broke with:

```
  Target "stylist" of type MODULE_LIBRARY may not be linked into another
  target.  One may link only to STATIC or SHARED libraries, or to executables
  with the ENABLE_EXPORTS property set.
```

I ended up with this:

```
find_package(Stylist REQUIRED)
include_directories(SYSTEM ${Stylist_INCLUDE_DIRS})
link_directories(${Stylist_LIBRARY_DIRS})

add_subdirectory(pybind11)
pybind11_add_module(_stylist SHARED ${SOURCES})
target_link_libraries(_stylist PRIVATE ${Stylist_LIBRARIES})
```

Which still fails:

```
>>> import _stylist
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: dynamic module does not define module export function (PyInit__stylist)
```

If I search for that error this appears to generally be caused by people compiling using Python 2 while trying to use the result with Python 3. That is not the case for me: Python 3 is used for the compilation as well.

issue_title: Get rid of the incremental nonce check in NettingChannelLibrary
issue_body:
Thanks to @stonecoldpat an issue was found with the incremental nonce check in [NettingChannelLibrary](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b14085c39c243d1684f0ddbed229abdac21e8229/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L274). 

In the case of a courtesy close a malicious Alice can provide a 0 value transfer for her latest transfer as the second argument to the `close()` function that has the maximum int value for the nonce.

This would render Bob unable to use `updateTransfer()` since any transaction would throw due to the `transfer.nonce` already having the maximum value.

The aforementioned check makes no sense anymore and should be removed, but some other things should be put in its place. This is an issue to implement:

### TODO

- [ ] Remove the [nonce check](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b14085c39c243d1684f0ddbed229abdac21e8229/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L274)

It currently serves no purpose other than making sure every transfer submitted is newer compared to the previous ones. When you close by providing the counterparty's transfer you are incentivized to provide the latest transfer anyway since it will contain the biggest transferred amount.

- [ ] Make sure that `updateTransfer()` can only be called once
- [ ] Get Rid of the courtesy close
Even though this is just a courtesy, it makes little sense and should be done away with.
Instead some sort of cooperative close (issue coming soon) should be implemented in its place

issue_title: Add support for non-inline CSS
issue_body:
Scour currently only supports inline CSS (and even that support might be broken in some cases as we might not always implement the proper inheritance rules).

A very desirable aim for Scour is therefore to properly parse global CSS.

There are multiple cases which should be investigated (from highest to lowest priority):
* inline CSS set using the `style` attribute  
  We already support most of this adequately, but investigating the use of a proper CSS parser might be a good first step. Unfortunately xml.dom.minidom does not offer this and most Python CSS parser implementations only parse CSS files but are not able to apply the parsed rules to a DOM tree (which we ultimately need).
* CSS embedded in the SVG document  using the `<style>` element
* externally referenced style sheets using `xml-stylesheet` processing instructions (https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-stylesheet/)  
  this involves additional complexity (and possibly security concerns) as it needs us to handle external files
* externally referenced style sheets that are not even referenced in the SVG itself but in an HTML document which might embed the processes SVG  
  This is obviously something Scour can not do without additional input from the user. We might allow to specify additional CSS files on command line which should be considered while scouring (should be easy after the previous point is solved).

Relevant section from the W3C spec:
https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG11/styling.html#StylingWithCSS

issue_title: Separate username configuration for remote host (instead of username@host)
issue_body:
`username@host` might be recommended for all `address` specification because when a ssh command is fired from a local host, it will use **local user name** as default. That is to say, a remote host definition will need to be changed if the local host has a different user name. To be safe, it would be recommended to specify user name for all remote hosts.

Now, we only use `ssh`, `rsync` and `rcp` in sos and we know `username@address` works for these commands. I am wondering if we should separate user as a separate configuration in case another communication command is needed that does not accept `user@address`.

issue_title: [local] section
issue_body:
Not sure if this is a good idea but I found myself doing this

```
---
%sosrun BIG
[global]
[BIG]
sos_run('small')
---
%run small
[???]
statement
[small]
---
```

The idea is that I have cells for small tasks, basically steps that I would like to experiment or run interactively, then I would like to run many such steps in `BIG` which has a bunch of `sos_run` calls. The problem here is that `statement` in `small` is supposed to be local, only used for `%run`, and I do not want to put it in `[global]` which would be merged to the global section. However, if I do not use `[global]` and put the statement directly there, the whole cell would be excluded from the notebook workflow because only cells with `[]` would be counted as part of the workflow.

Solution 1:
Define `[local]` section that would be excluded from the global workflow.

Solution 2:
Allow the use of 
```
statement
[step]
...
```
and use another method to decide if `step` should be part of the notebook workflow.

issue_title: sos publish or upload command
issue_body:
We need to make the archival of sos workflow and notebooks extremely easy so a `sos publish` command should be in the radar.

The SoS script or notebook should contain specific fields such as keyword and author etc. The SoS publish command will collect those and serves as meta information for the notebook. (a bit like `python setup.py upload` for pip).

The SoS configuration file should contain server information and user credentials to the jnbinder or other workflow archival service.

Then the `sos publish` or `sos upload` command will publish the script or notebook to the server.

I suppose the first step would be the format of meta information. In SoS script, meta information can be in plain text but should follow some specific format. In SoS notebook, we should have a magic and/or button for users to enter meta information, which will then be saved to notebook meta information section.

issue_title: Do not use pipenv for packages specified in tox.ini
issue_body:
I've been trying to convert one of my Django apps to use pipenv and I'm hitting issues with tox because, as I switch to it, I lose the ability for tox to pick up the app's dependencies which were previously in setup.cfg, setup.py or requirements.txt.

I tried this plugin hoping it would help, and maybe I'm misunderstanding what problem it's trying to solves, so if I am, forgive this ticket, though there is still an unsolved problem then.

Here's a typical Django app using tox and pipenv:

1. Dependencies for the project itself are defined in Pipfile
2. A lockfile is distributed alongside the Pipfile
3. tox.ini will look something like this:

```ini
[tox]
skipsdist = True
envlist = py36-django{20,master}, flake8, mypy

[testenv]
commands = pytest --showlocals {posargs}
deps =
	django20: Django>=2.0,<2.1
	djangomaster: https://github.com/django/django/archive/master.tar.gz
	pytest
	pytest-django
	coveralls

[testenv:flake8]
skip_install = True
commands =
	flake8
deps =
	flake8
	flake8-isort
	flake8-quotes

[testenv:mypy]
commands =
	mypy --ignore-missing-imports
deps =
	mypy
```

Now there's a fundamental conflict between tox and pipenv here. pipenv will install what's in the production lockfile. tox will attempt to install django 2.0, then django 2.1, django master, etc. tox will also install flake8 and mypy for the other environments, which will add them to the Pipfile and the lockfile. The `--skip-lock` argument to pipenv will prevent the lockfile from being updated at least, but they still shouldn't be added to the pipfile.

Fundamentally, all pipenv does is call pip and update the pipfile/lockfile accordingly. We want pipenv to *create* the environment, but not to install the packages provided by tox otherwise you will end up with modified pipfiles.

So my suggestion is something like this:
1. Call pipenv to create the initial virtualenv (including the packages) unless `skip_install = True`.
2. Afterwards, let tox use pip as it normally would to install the extra packages specified in tox deps.

Does that make sense, or am I completely off the mark?

"""

  of the image. Default is 0, i.e no padding. If a sequence of length
  4 is provided, it is used to pad left, top, right, bottom borders
  respectively.
"""
    def __init__(self, size, padding=0, pad_if_needed=False):
        if isinstance(size, numbers.Number):
            self.size = (int(size), int(size))
        else:
            self.size = size
        self.padding = padding
        self.pad_if_needed = pad_if_needed
```
then, when it is called, this argument is compared with 0:
```python
    def __call__(self, img):
        """
        Args:
            img (PIL Image): Image to be cropped.
        Returns:
            PIL Image: Cropped image.
        """
        if self.padding > 0:
            img = F.pad(img, self.padding)
```
this is okay in python 2 where this comparison **always** returns True. but in python 3, comparison between tuple and int is illegal, and thus it will raise an type error.


P.S. there is another confusing feature. when I use `RandomCrop((128, 32), padding=(16, 4))`, I obviously expect each entry of *padding* should match the corresponding entry in *size* (i.e. the height 128 should be padded with 16, and width 32 padded with 4), but in fact it is inverse (128 with 4, 32 with 16). I think this is weird...

issue_title: Real controller interface/service
issue_body:
At the moment, our deployment orchestration is incredibly naive.  There are known issues around the way state is managed (see #101 and #111), the source of truth is wrong (see #142), the extensibility points aren't there (like logging), and all deployments are driven by a synchronous CLI, and there is no notion of a service or asynchronous deployment.

This work item tracks the significant design and architecture work, and then more modest implementation, to build this right.  It should likely entail (at least) these steps:

* Create a deployment controller interface that can be remoted over a gRPC connection
* Implement this interface for the local, synchronous CLI scenarios
* Implement this interface for the remote, service scenarios (and support asynchronous)
* Add a command to check the status of an asynchronous deployment
* Create a way for the CLI to specify the remote service
* Add a way to configure an environment to use one particular remote service by default

In addition, we should address #142 and we hope #101 and #111 are addressed by these items.

issue_title: What's the best way to control which GPUs a worker can use?
issue_body:
Right now, we allow tasks to specify that they require GPUs by including it in the decorator, e.g.,

```python
@ray.remote(num_gpus=2)
def f():
  ...
```

#302 introduces the same syntax for actors, e.g.,

```python
@ray.actor(num_gpus=3)
class Foo(object):
  ...
```

So how does the function `f` actually know which GPUs to use?

With CPUs, the OS will try to balance things between CPUs, so it's less critical to get it right. That said, the local scheduler (or whoever) could set the affinity of different worker processes for different CPUs to control which CPUs each worker can use. Is there an analogue of all this for GPUs?

GPUs seem different from CPUs. The burden of choosing *which* GPU to use is often placed on the programmer, not on the OS. For example, the standard way of controlling which GPUs TensorFlow uses is to set the environment variable `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`, e.g., something like `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,3,4` before running `tf.Session()`. Once you create a session, TensorFlow will reserve a bunch of memory on all visible GPUs. I'm not sure how specific the concept of using GPU device IDs is to TensorFlow or if it is general.

We can expose a method `ray.get_gpu_ids()` that could be called inside any task or any actor and would return the IDs (e.g., `[0, 3, 4]`) of the GPUs that that process is allowed to use. This assumes that environment variables can be set from within Python. In the case of TensorFlow, that works, e.g., we can do `os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = ",".join([str(i) for i in ray.get_gpu_ids()])` or something like that from Python (e.g., within an actor constructor) **as long as we do it before we run `tf.Session()`**. But you could imagine a scenario or a different library where the environment variable has to be set BEFORE the worker process is created (or before the library is imported). In that case, there are other options (e.g., having the local scheduler set the environment variable), but they all seem awful (actually, I ran into such a situation recently where I had to set an environment variable like `DISPLAY=:99` before importing a library because otherwise the library crashed when looking for an X server and brought down the worker).

issue_title: The ActorReconstruction.testManyLocalSchedulersDying test in actor_test.py sometimes hangs.
issue_body:
If you run the following in a loop (on one of our Jenkins workers), one of the runs will eventually hang. This happens in Travis a lot as well.

```
python test/actor_test.py ActorReconstruction.testManyLocalSchedulersDying
```

Connecting another driver to one of the hanging instances, I see the following. Running

```python
import ray
ray.init(redis_address="127.0.0.1:27196")
tt = ray.global_state.task_table()
states = [v['State'] for k, v in tt.items()]
[(s, states.count(s)) for s in set(states)]
```

the last line prints

```
[(16, 694), (32, 143), (4, 20), (8, 2)]
```

so a number of tasks are in the `QUEUED` state. I checked and they seem to be queued at the live local scheduler (not a dead one). The number `4` corresponds to `QUEUED`. See 

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/b1660c4edfcbfec5645103eaa0aad58a255015fa/src/common/task.h#L336-L352

I tried adding a commit to dispatch all tasks on a timer https://github.com/ray-project/ray/compare/master...robertnishihara:dispatchontimer, but that didn't change the behavior, tasks are still queued. I'm not totally sure what the problem is, but maybe the task dependencies or something are preventing some tasks from getting executed.

cc @stephanie-wang

SQLAlchemy allows you to construct SQL queries while preventing injection:

from sqlalchemy import text
